title: Multilayer Hydrogels as Muscle-Like Actuators
abstract:
Communications
288
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 2000
0935-9648/00/0402-0288 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 2000, 12, No. 4
riers in the 6T wire.[3] Holes are trapped by defects in the
crystal, which reduces the number of mobile carriers and
hinders the movement of untrapped positive-charge car-
riers. Charge trapping can be reversed, however, by apply-
ing a positive-gate voltage (backbiasing) to force trapped
holes out of trap states via electrostatic repulsion. As seen
from the trace on the right-hand side of Figure 3B, hole
current was temporarily restored to nearly 100 % of its
original value by applying a sufficient backbias. However,
trapping immediately resumed when the normal operating
voltages were re-established.
In conclusion, we have shown that we can use AFM
nanoshaving to fabricate functional semiconducting wires
from the molecular semiconductor sexithiophene. Because
there is nothing particularly special about sexithiophene
per se, there is no reason why the method cannot be ap-
plied to other organic semiconductors, provided they are
soft enough for an AFM tip to carve. We have shown that
wires carved to 300 and 70 nm widths (Figures 1B and 1D,
respectively) are obtainable, but based on our work and
that of others,[5a] we believe that it would be possible to
carve wires as thin as 20 nm. Through photoconductivity
and temperature-dependent transport measurements, we
have demonstrated that the electrical properties of the
wires are consistent with what we have observed for single
grains of 6T. Finally, we have investigated time-dependent
transport in the wires, and our data were consistent with
our previous assertion[3] that 6T undergoes reversible trap-
ping of positive-charge carriers.
The ability to create long, narrow wires of organic semi-
conductors can be further exploited in transport studies.
For example, we estimate that using the gate electrode we
can controllably induce 100±500 positive charges in a
20 nm wide, 500 nm long 6T wire. Fabrication of thin wires
thus provides opportunities to observe discrete events such
as charge trapping or Coulomb effects in organic semicon-
ductors, as has been reported for carbon nanotubes.[13] Such
studies will be the subject of future work.
Received: September 8, 1999
Final version: November 11, 1999

Multilayer Hydrogels as Muscle-Like
Actuators**
By Zengshe Liu and Paul Calvert*
An electrically driven muscle-like actuator has been con-
structed from a series of layers of cross-linked polyacrylic
acid and polyacrylamide hydrogels. In air, a uniform block
of gel will bend in response to an applied field or will expel
water and contract. The gel stack changes shape as water is
driven from one material to the other. Compared to pre-
vious systems, this has the advantage of providing a linear
motion, rather than bending, while retaining the water
within the actuator.
There is a clear need for actuators with properties com-
parable to human muscle, especially for robot arms. Motor-
ized or hydraulic arms tend to be heavy and clumsy. Hu-
±
[*] Dr. P. Calvert, Dr. Z. Liu[+]
Department of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Arizona
Tucson, AZ 85721 (USA)
[+] Present address: Oil Chemical Research, NCAUR, ARS, USDA,
Peoria, IL 61604, USA.
[**] We would like to thank the Army Research Office for support of this
work.
 man muscle is driven by chemical energy, whereas an artifi-
cial muscle ought to respond to an electrical impulse by a
change in length. There are many candidate materials but
all have considerable drawbacks at the moment. Piezoelec-
tric actuators have a rapid response but the total strain is
small, less than 0.1 % and, as a result, complicated linkages
are needed to get significant motion. Electrically conduct-
ing polymers show volume changes of a few percent but
this is still much below that needed for a muscle. Shape
memory alloys are promising but the response is slow and
the cycle life limited. A soft-polymer actuator is the ob-
vious analog for a muscle for fast but gentle manipulations.
Hydrogels have long been studied as possible actua-
tors.[1±4] A piece of hydrogel, immersed in an aqueous solu-
tion, can be induced to change volume by any stimulus that
will change the polymer±liquid interaction and hence the
degree of swelling. Thus a small temperature increase can
cause an abrupt volume change, while an increase in pH
will ionize polyacrylic acid and cause it to swell.[5,6] Similar
changes can be induced by addition of a poorer solvent or a
change in ionic strength. Several groups have produced
muscle analogs using chemically driven volume changes
but the need for a pumped-fluid driving system makes the
approach impractical.
Ionizable gels do also respond to electrical fields and a
number of demonstration devices have been produced.[7]
In one example, a strip of gel is immersed in water between
two external electrodes and responds to the field by bend-
ing. The chemical origin of the bending and the direction of
bending are dependent on the whether the electrodes are
contacting the gel or are immersed in the surrounding liq-
uid.[8] This system is also unsatisfactory as muscle, both be-
cause a bending action is harder to couple to motion of a
machine and because water may be flowing in and out of
the gel, making it difficult to operate in air.
In a previous paper,[9] we reported on the swelling of
layered
gels,
with
cross-linked
polyacrylamide
layers
stacked on polyacrylic acid layers. As the stacks are moved
from acid into base, the polyacrylic acid layers swell strongly.
We expected that these structures would show strong bend-
ing as the polyacrylic acid layers expanded but that the poly-
acrylamide layers would not. In fact, the stacks responded to
a change from acid to base by swelling with little bending.
We suggested that the much weaker polyacrylamide±water
interaction allows this gel to take up excess water with little
energy change, when it experiences biaxial tension as the at-
tached polyacrylic acid swells strongly. The system can be
thought of as a bilayer strip of a stiff material, which under-
goes strong expansion and contraction, attached to a soft
material, which is inert. When the stiff material expands, the
soft material is simply stretched.
This coupling of the gel layers suggested that an electri-
cally-driven actuator could be built where the response to a
field between embedded electrodes would be internal
water transfer and a uniaxial contraction or expansion
rather than bending.
Initially, we built stacks of six gel layers: two of polyacrylic
acid gel followed by four of polyacrylamide gel with
embedded palladium wire electrodes between layers 1 and 2
and between layers 5 and 6. The shrinkage of these gels was
studied in response to a field of up to 6 V. The response of
the system is very dependent on the state of ionization and
the ionic strength in the acid gel. The stack was prepared by
immersion in dilute base to allow the acid gel to ionize fully
and to swell the acrylamide gel to equilibrium. It was then
washed in deionized water to reduce the electrolyte concen-
tration. The stack was then treated for 5 minutes with dilute
acid (0.25 M HCl) to bring the acid gel to a low degree of
ionization. Previous titrations showed that swelling changed
with ionization most rapidly in this range. Thus the gel
would also be most responsive to current in this region.
From this partly-swollen state, the gel was removed from
the buffer, placed on a dry plate and a potential of 6 volts,
with the acrylic acid side positive, was applied for 12 min-
utes to the embedded wires. The current flowing during this
process was about 8 mA/cm2 The stack contracted by about
50 % along the x and y axes, as is shown in Figure 1. During
this process, water did exude from the system with a weight
loss of 20±30 %. Since the area of the sample changed by a
factor of four and the weight decreased by about 30 %, the
thickness of the stack must also increase.
Fig. 1. Four layer gel: A(o)/Wire/A/M(o)/Wire/M. Top after buffering at pH
5, washing and brief acid treatment. Bottom after 6 V applied for 12 min-
utes, A side positive. A polyacrylic acid gel, M: polyacrylamide gel. (o) open
mesh layer, other layers solid.
We then sought a system which would allow a more mod-
est contraction without loss of water so that the material

riers in the 6T wire.[3] Holes are trapped by defects in the
crystal, which reduces the number of mobile carriers and
hinders the movement of untrapped positive-charge car-
riers. Charge trapping can be reversed, however, by apply-
ing a positive-gate voltage (backbiasing) to force trapped
holes out of trap states via electrostatic repulsion. As seen
from the trace on the right-hand side of Figure 3B, hole
current was temporarily restored to nearly 100 % of its
original value by applying a sufficient backbias. However,

In conclusion, we have shown that we can use AFM
nanoshaving to fabricate functional semiconducting wires
from the molecular semiconductor sexithiophene. Because
there is nothing particularly special about sexithiophene
per se, there is no reason why the method cannot be ap-
plied to other organic semiconductors, provided they are
soft enough for an AFM tip to carve. We have shown that
wires carved to 300 and 70 nm widths (Figures 1B and 1D,
respectively) are obtainable, but based on our work and
that of others,[5a] we believe that it would be possible to
carve wires as thin as 20 nm. Through photoconductivity
and temperature-dependent transport measurements, we
have demonstrated that the electrical properties of the
wires are consistent with what we have observed for single
grains of 6T. Finally, we have investigated time-dependent
transport in the wires, and our data were consistent with
our previous assertion[3] that 6T undergoes reversible trap-
ping of positive-charge carriers.
The ability to create long, narrow wires of organic semi-
conductors can be further exploited in transport studies.
For example, we estimate that using the gate electrode we
can controllably induce 100±500 positive charges in a
20 nm wide, 500 nm long 6T wire. Fabrication of thin wires
thus provides opportunities to observe discrete events such
as charge trapping or Coulomb effects in organic semicon-
ductors, as has been reported for carbon nanotubes.[13] Such
studies will be the subject of future work.

had the potential to run reversibly in a dry environment.
An alternative electrode system was also needed, as there
was tendency of the gel to burn around the wire electrodes
and for the electrodes to pull out with these large shape
An arrangement was developed with carbon foil elec-
trodes placed above and below a gel stack. This system was
swollen in 0.1 M phthalate buffer at pH 5 and then re-
moved from the buffer and placed between the electrodes
on a dry plate. The thickness change on applying a poten-
tial was then followed by recording the deflection on a ver-
tical scale of a laser reflected off the horizontal top surface
of the stack. The recorded deflection is then twice the
The results of this are shown in Figure 2. It can be seen
that the thickness changes by about 10 % in 1 min at 3 V.
We were concerned that this height change might be due to
bending, rather than a thickness change. No difference was
seen between height changes measured by reflection form
the edge and center of the stack, so this is a simple thick-
ness change, occurring as the acid gel contracts along the x
and y directions and displaces water into the acrylamide
gel, which expands along the z-direction. A sample was
subjected to a series of 10 cycles, with current on for 1 min,
off for 1 min, reversed for 1 min then off for 1 min, as
shown in Figure 3. These results show that the 10 % thick-
ness change can be cycled. There are evidently changes,
probably in ion distribution, that occur in the first cycle,
and evaporative drying would be a problem in these un-
sealed systems over longer times.
The action of these gels is achieved by coupling poly-
acrylic acid, which has a strong affinity for water in the ion-
ized state, with polyacrylamide, which has a lesser water
affinity, as shown schematically in Figure 4. Thus the poly-
acrylamide may take up water from the polyacrylate as it
neutralizes to polyacrylic acid and shrinks. In addition the
polyacrylamide must have a low elastic modulus, compared
to the polyacrylic acid, so that it deforms with the poly-
acrylic acid as it expands and contracts. The modulus of
polyacrylamide, was previously measured at 390 kPa fully
swollen, compared to 650 kPa for the polyacrylic acid, fully
swollen in base and at a similar crosslink density.[9] For the
experiments described here the crosslink density of the

This is not yet the ideal muscle system. The current
drives the shape change through a pH gradient which arises
from electrolysis of water, which cannot be reversible in a
closed system. The gas formed also causes instabilities in
our system. Other workers have experimented with the re-
lease of calcium ions from polypyrrole as an alternative sys-
tem for swelling and deswelling the gels without electroly-
sis of water.[10] The gels are also mechanically weak, but
much stronger gels can be made by combining polyvinylal-
cohol with polyacrylic acid.[11]
As outlined above, an ideal muscle should show an over-
all expansion or contraction along one axis in response to
an applied field, with no overall volume change. This can-
not be achieved with a single centrosymmetric material but
is possible with an asymmetric series of layers. We have
shown that stacked combinations of gels can be made to
give linear contraction in response to applied electric field.
In essence the asymmetry of gel stack balances the asym-
metry of the applied field to allow a symmetric response.
Other swollen polymer combinations should be capable of
Gels were made from solutions of acrylamide or acrylic acid at 200±
400 g/L of water. The cross-linking agent was methylenebisacrylamide at
levels from 20±50 g/L. For the acrylamide, the initiator was potassium per-
sulfate, 0.25 g/L and the accelerator was tetramethylethylenediamine
(TEMED) 3.6 g/L. For the acrylic acid, the catalyst system was a potassium
persulfate (1.14 g)-potassium metabisulfite (0.94 g) redox couple. Fumed
silica (210 g/L) was added as a physical gelling agent and the pH adjusted to
Gel stacks were made by extrusion freeform fabrication. Acrylic mono-
mer, water and catalyst is extruded from a syringe through a fine (0.2±1 mm
diameter) nozzle which writes on a heated substrate. The composition of the
gel can be changed by swapping syringes or by using a Y-junction between
two syringe feeds. To prevent the monomer solution from flowing off the
substrate before curing, 8 wt.-% of fumed silica is added to the solution, to
give it a toothpaste-like rheology. The solution cures to cross-linked gel by
thermally activated free radical polymerization within a few minutes of
being deposited on the plate. 6-layer stacks were formed into bars 5 cm
´ 0.5 cm (wide) ´ 0.4 cm (thick), before swelling. A typical stack would have
a polyacrylic acid face with 1±5 (x) layers and a polyacrylamide face of (6±x)
layers with a combination of dense and open-mesh layers (written as a series
of spaced lines). When used, wire electrodes were usually placed between
layers 1 and 2, and between 5 and 6.
This system allows interdiffusion and bonding between layers, which pro-
vides good stress transfer during the swelling processes. The early onset of
gelation and the physical gelling agent prevent extensive mixing between
layers. This was shown by cutting apart multilayer stacks and measuring the
response of individual layers to acid and base.

By Suresh Baskaran,* Jun Liu, Karel Domansky,

The semiconductor industry is currently targeting new in-
termetal dielectric films with dielectric constant k < 2.5,
and it is anticipated that as the packing density of metal
lines on the semiconductors continues to increase, interlev-
el dielectric films with k < 2.0 will soon be required. Porous
silica films with nanometer-scale porosity are potentially
useful as low dielectric constant intermetal materials in
advanced semiconductor devices. Nanoporous silica films
can be synthesized from solution precursors in two ways:
